divergence: The Black Death targeted only landlords
story_angle: exposé of a scandal that every official proudly confirms on the record
headline: Plague Enters Third Year of Afflicting Only Landlords; Crown Declares Housing Crisis as Rents Reach Zero
deck: The Guild of Surviving Landlords, eleven members strong, petitions the King for a bailout, citing "unprecedented and frankly hurtful tenant confidence."
article:
NETHER WALLOP — It is now three summers since the Great Mortality came among us, and the learned doctors of Oxford confirm what every ploughman has whispered over his suspiciously excellent new feather bed: the pestilence takes only landlords. Rents across the realm have fallen from three shillings to a cheerful wave, and the Crown, after consulting the eleven landlords who remain, has declared a housing crisis.

The mechanism of the plague remains a mystery to physicians, though patterns have emerged. The royal college reports the contagion spreads through the touching of rent ledgers, the raising of rents, and, in one well-documented case in Shropshire, the phrase "that's just market rates." Peasants who handle the same ledgers to burn them report feeling, if anything, invigorated.

The Guild of Surviving Landlords has petitioned His Majesty for relief, proposing a scheme of "posthumous rent tithes" under which tenants would owe back-payment to their late landlords' estates, payable to the Guild, for safekeeping, forever. Guild spokesman Lord Osric, addressing the press from a safe distance of one county, clarified that he is no longer a landlord but a "shelter facilitator," a title he invented on Tuesday and has already survived longer than three colleagues who laughed at it.

The Church, for its part, remains divided on whether the pestilence is divine punishment or, as the young friars of Canterbury argue, "the most precisely targeted miracle on record." A synod convened to settle the question adjourned early when it was discovered the abbey holds forty tenant farms, and the abbot developed a cough of remarkable political timing.

Meanwhile the common folk endure as best they can. "It has been hard," admitted Wat Tyler the Younger, speaking from the great hall of a manor he entered through the front door for the first time in his life. "There are twelve of us in this house now. There are also forty rooms. We are managing."
author: Brother Aldwin the Approximately Literate, Court Chronicler

oped.title: The Plague Is Punishing Ambition Itself
oped.author: Lord Osric of the Four Remaining Manors, President, Guild of Surviving Landlords
oped.body: Let us speak plainly, as I now do all things: plainly, quickly, and from a distance. This pestilence is no judgment upon landlords. It is a judgment upon ambition, upon enterprise, upon the noble instinct to own a thing another man needs and charge him for it. Today it comes for the landlord; tomorrow, mark me, it comes for the miller, the moneylender, any man of vision. My solution is simple: let every peasant be made a landlord of every other peasant, in a great circle, so that the plague, confused, must either take everyone or admit the whole arrangement was personal. I write this from my former tenant's cottage, where I now lodge for reasons of health. He has not asked for rent. I find this suspicious, and also merciful, and I have never slept better, which is its own kind of shame.

classifieds.text: Entire manor, haunted only by opportunity. Rent: whatever feels right. Enquire of any peasant inside; there are several and they are in excellent spirits.
The Guild of Surviving Landlords seeks a food taster, and separately, a ledger taster. Wages generous. Predecessors unavailable for reference.
Comely widow, 34, seeks husband. Must be kind, hale, and hold no property whatsoever, for actuarial reasons.
The village of Nether Wallop is FULL. Please enjoy one of our many other free manors. The one with the moat is nice.

panels.caption: A landlord disguises himself as a peasant to escape the pestilence.
His tenants, moved by the effort, offer to charge him rent to complete the disguise.
He haggles. The plague hears him counting coins.

joke.setup: What is the difference between the plague and a landlord?
joke.punchline: The plague inspects the property only once.
joke.text: What is the difference between the plague and a landlord? The plague inspects the property only once.

sponsor_ads.headline: Honest Wat's Peasant Disguises
sponsor_ads.body: Smocks so authentic your own ledger disowns you. Fitted while you flee.

sponsor_ads.headline: St. Roderick's Absolution & Storage
sponsor_ads.body: Confess your deposits. We keep them in a very holy hole and remember almost where.
sponsor_ads.tagline: The Church takes only a tithe of a tithe.

sponsor_ads.headline: The Rent-Free Herald
sponsor_ads.body: A broadsheet for the new homeowner. All sixteen thousand of you.

sponsor_ads.headline: Join the Guild of Surviving Landlords

real_world_echo: The housing affordability crisis and landlord discourse — inverted into a world where rent, not housing, is what collapses.

stories.headline: Commons Games Enter Fourth Glorious Week, as Nobody Owns the Field
stories.deck: The trophy is the meadow itself, which each champion politely and urgently declines.
stories.article:
The Great Commons Games continued their fourth week upon the meadow at Nether Wallop, there being no landlord alive to end them. Events include hurdling the old boundary fences, long-distance strolling across formerly private acreage, and a wrestling match over who must accept the deed to the field, this year's grand prize.

Wat the Swift, victorious in three events, refused the deed at once, citing his health. It was pressed upon the runner-up, who fled. The deed currently rests atop the maypole, where the Church says it can do no harm, probably.

stories.headline: Ledger-Burning Guild Reports Record Warmth, Record Applications
stories.deck: The Church convenes to determine whether heat derived from rent records constitutes usury.
stories.article:
The newly chartered Guild of Ledger-Burners reports its most prosperous season, with rent records providing the parish's warmth through the spring chill. "Fine, dry burning," said Guildmaster Tilda, feeding the flames a particularly overdue account. "The interest columns go up like kindling. It's the compounding."

The Church has convened a synod to rule whether warmth extracted from interest is itself interest, and therefore sin. The synod meets beside the fire, for comfort, and is expected to deliberate until the ledgers run out.
